summary:
The 1948 Arab-Israeli War began on May 15, 1948, when Egypt, Jordan, Iraq, Syria, and Lebanon invaded the newly declared [[event:2672|State of Israel]], one day after its independence proclamation. The Arab states refused to accept the UN partition plan and sought to prevent the establishment of a Jewish state. Initial Arab advances in the south and north threatened the new state's survival, but Israeli forces — drawing on trained Haganah personnel and absorbing weapons from Czechoslovakia — stabilized the front and gradually gained the upper hand through several rounds of fighting.

The war unfolded in phases punctuated by UN-brokered truces. Israeli forces used the truces to reorganize, receive arms shipments, and train new recruits. By the autumn offensive of October 1948, Israeli forces had dramatically expanded their territorial control. By the war's end in March 1949, Israel controlled approximately 78 percent of the territory of mandatory Palestine, including areas allocated to the proposed Arab state under the UN partition plan.

Jordan's Arab Legion, the most professional Arab army, captured and occupied the West Bank and East Jerusalem, including the Old City. Egypt retained the Gaza Strip. These territories would remain under Jordanian and Egyptian control respectively until the [[event:2686|Six-Day War]] of 1967. The approximately 700,000 Palestinian Arabs who fled or were expelled during the war — the Nakba or catastrophe — were prevented from returning to their homes, creating a refugee population whose descendants numbered in the millions by the twenty-first century.

Armistice agreements signed separately with Egypt, Jordan, Lebanon, and Syria in 1949 established the Green Line — the armistice demarcation line — as Israel's de facto borders for nearly two decades. The armistices were not peace treaties, and all the Arab states maintained a formal state of war with Israel. The unresolved questions of Palestinian refugees, borders, and sovereignty became permanent fault lines in the region's politics.
